knowledge_base.topic: Making AI Deterministic
knowledge_base.content: Most AI models hallucinate in production because of one main reason: too much freedom. See, there are two types of systems: deterministic and non-deterministic. Deterministic is where you give an input and you expect the same output everytime. For example, you make an API call. Now, 99% of the times, you will get the same output. Whereas Non-deterministic means everytime you give the same input, you will not get the same output, or you may not get the same output. And when you're building production-ready AI agents, your job is to make AI agent as deterministic as possible. You can do it through multiple techniques: By lowering the temperature, Forcing structured outputs (like JSON), Adding examples in the system prompt, Limiting the model's scope. And there are many others.
key_points: Two types of systems: deterministic vs non-deterministic
Lower the temperature
Force structured outputs (JSON)
Add examples in system prompt
Limit model's scope

knowledge_base.topic: Response Caching
knowledge_base.content: I've seen startups burn thousands of dollars because of one mistake. They don't store AI responses. Especially in customer-facing AI systems, users ask the same questions again and again. But instead of reusing answers, they hit the LLM every single time. The smarter approach is you should store high-quality responses in your database, embed them, and run semantic search on every new query. If the similarity is high enough, then directly return responses from database. This alone can save lot of costs because once you've a good database size, you'll hardly need AI model. But remember to keep cleaning and revalidating the database as your business logics change.
key_points: Users ask same questions repeatedly
Store high-quality responses in database
Embed responses for semantic search
Return from cache when similarity is high
Clean and revalidate database regularly

knowledge_base.topic: Grounding
knowledge_base.content: A lot of AI hallucinations can be stopped with one simple technique - Grounding. Grounding basically means not letting the model answer on its own. Force it to answer from your data. You do grounding using: Database records, Internal documents, APIs. For example, lets say user asks about refund status. Without context, AI will definitely hallucinate. Whereas if you provide API access to llm, AI first calls your payments API, gets the real status, and then responds. You can to implement this in production through various ways - You can use RAG, You can force model to cite sources, You should add "I don't know" triggers when confidence is low.
key_points: Don't let model answer on its own
Use database records, internal docs, APIs
Implement RAG
Force model to cite sources
Add 'I don't know' triggers when confidence is low

knowledge_base.topic: Rate Limiting
knowledge_base.content: Many developers don't talk about this until it's too late. I'm talking about rate limiting. Without rate limiting: One user can spam unlimited requests, One bug can loop API calls, And your bill explodes overnight. Every system you build must have: User-level rate limiting, IP-level rate limiting. And for high-throughput systems, you can even implement the token bucket algorithm. Save this before it's too late.
key_points: One user can spam unlimited requests
One bug can loop API calls
Bill explodes overnight
User-level rate limiting
IP-level rate limiting
Token bucket algorithm for high-throughput

knowledge_base.topic: Error Handling and Fallbacks
knowledge_base.content: Many AI developers completely ignore error handling. If your AI fails, you must have fallbacks and alerting mechanisms. If your feature is fully AI-dependent, you need to design for the worst-case scenario where the model is down, slow, or returns garbage. The fallback won't be as good as AI, but it will keep users on the platform. You can even implement graceful degradation where if AI fails, return cached responses from your database. Next, you must have a monitoring system running 24/7 that should notify you immediately in case of any mishaps. Lastly, everything depends on how you present it to the user. Instead of showing empty screen, display clearly what went wrong and suggest alternatives to the user.
key_points: Design for worst-case scenario
Implement graceful degradation
Return cached responses as fallback
24/7 monitoring system
Clear error messages to users

knowledge_base.topic: Prompt Versioning
knowledge_base.content: This one skill can massively improve your AI responses: prompt versioning. See, nobody writes perfect prompts on the first try. It takes multiple iterations to reach a prompt that consistently gives the best results. That's exactly where prompt versioning helps. Every time you change a prompt, keep the history, test it against edge cases, and compare the outputs with older versions. Most people get shocked when I say this: you should store your prompts in Git, not in Google Docs. Treat prompts like code. You can even run A/B testing: send real traffic to different prompt versions and measure which one performs better.
key_points: Nobody writes perfect prompts first try
Keep prompt history
Test against edge cases
Store prompts in Git, not Google Docs
Run A/B testing on prompts

knowledge_base.topic: Human-in-the-Loop
knowledge_base.content: Whenever you're building AI-automated systems, there's one question you must ask yourself: Should AI make the final decision? And most of the time, the answer is no. That's where human-in-the-loop comes in. Human-in-the-loop means: AI assists, but humans validate when it actually matters. One of the simplest ways to implement this is by using confidence thresholds. For example: if the model's confidence is above 70%, let AI handle it automatically. If it's below that, route it to a human for review. This is especially important for Financial decisions, Hiring, Medical or legal systems, And Customer support escalations.
key_points: AI assists, humans validate
Use confidence thresholds (e.g., 70%)
Critical for: financial, hiring, medical, legal
Auto-handle high confidence
Route low confidence to humans

knowledge_base.topic: Latency Optimization
knowledge_base.content: If your AI takes too long to respond, your users will leave. It doesn't matter how accurate the response is because half of the users are gone by then. So how do you actually reduce AI latency in production? First: streaming. In 90% of real products, you should never wait for the full response. Start showing tokens as they're generated. Second: cache common queries. If people ask the same thing repeatedly, just reuse them. Third: parallel processing. You can run independent tasks parallely. Fourth: use faster models for simple tasks. Not everything needs your most expensive model. And finally: UI matters. Show "Thinking…" with animations. Show skeleton responses instead of boring loader.
key_points: Use streaming - show tokens as generated
Cache common queries
Parallel processing for independent tasks
Use faster models for simple tasks
Show animations and skeleton responses

knowledge_base.topic: Model Routing
knowledge_base.content: This one technique alone can save you a lot of money and time. It's called model routing. Instead of sending every query to your best and most expensive model, first try to analyze intent. Is this a simple FAQ? Or is this a product query, order status, payment issue? For simple FAQs, you don't need heavy AI at all. Use cached responses, or route to a lighter, cheaper model. This alone can reduce massive cost and latency. But for complex queries like product logic, order tracking, or payments you need grounding, RAG, tool calling. So you route those to a more powerful model to improve response quality. In production, create different cohorts of use cases, and define exactly how each one should be handled.
key_points: Analyze intent first
Simple FAQs → cached responses or light model
Complex queries → powerful model with RAG
Create cohorts of use cases
Can reduce costs by 60%+

knowledge_base.topic: Input Validation
knowledge_base.content: Most AI failures happen before the model even runs. I'm talking about input validation. Without proper validation: Malicious prompts can jailbreak your AI, Edge cases crash your system, Invalid data gets processed. Every AI system needs: Schema validation for structured inputs, Content filtering for harmful requests, Length limits to prevent token overflow, Type checking before processing. For production systems, add a validation layer that checks: Is the input format correct? Does it contain prohibited content? Is it within acceptable size limits? Reject bad inputs early, before they reach your expensive AI model.
key_points: Most failures happen before model runs
Schema validation for structured inputs
Content filtering for harmful requests
Length limits to prevent token overflow
Reject bad inputs early

knowledge_base.topic: Token Usage Optimization
knowledge_base.content: Your AI bill is probably 10x higher than it should be. Why? Because you're sending too many tokens. The biggest mistake I see: Sending entire conversation history every single time. Instead, implement context compression: Summarize old messages, Keep only relevant context, Use sliding window for chat history, Remove redundant information. Also optimize your prompts: Remove verbose instructions, Use abbreviations where possible, Cache system prompts, Batch similar requests. One startup reduced their token usage by 70% just by implementing smart context management.
key_points: Biggest mistake: sending entire conversation history
Implement context compression
Summarize old messages
Use sliding window for chat history
Can reduce token usage by 70%

knowledge_base.topic: Output Validation
knowledge_base.content: Your AI gave a response. Now what? Most developers just send it directly to users. Big mistake. You need output validation layers: Format checking - Is the JSON valid? Is the structure correct? Content moderation - Does it contain harmful content? Hallucination detection - Are the facts verifiable? Quality scoring - Does it meet minimum standards? Implement guardrails: Parse and validate structured outputs, Run toxicity detection, Check against known facts, Score response quality. If validation fails, either retry with modified prompt or fall back to safe default. Never blindly trust AI outputs in production.
key_points: Never blindly trust AI outputs
Format checking (JSON, structure)
Content moderation
Hallucination detection
Quality scoring
Retry or fallback on validation failure

knowledge_base.topic: Context Window Management
knowledge_base.content: Running out of context? Your architecture is wrong. Here's what most people miss about context windows: The model doesn't need EVERYTHING. Smart context management means: Prioritize recent messages, Summarize older context, Extract key information only, Use retrieval instead of stuffing. For long conversations: Keep last N messages in full, Summarize messages N to 2N, Index older messages in vector DB, Retrieve only relevant history. For RAG systems: Chunk documents smartly, Retrieve only top-K relevant chunks, Re-rank retrieved content. Context is expensive. Use it wisely.
key_points: Model doesn't need everything
Keep last N messages in full
Summarize older messages
Index old messages in vector DB
Retrieve only relevant history
Can save 60-70% of tokens

knowledge_base.topic: Multi-Model Strategy
knowledge_base.content: Stop using one model for everything. Production systems need multiple models working together. Here's how the best teams do it: Classification model first - cheap, fast, routes requests. Small model for simple tasks - handles 70% of queries. Large model for complex tasks - only 30% of traffic. Specialized models for specific domains. Example architecture: User query → Classifier → Simple? → Small model (fast, cheap), Complex? → Large model (slow, expensive), Domain-specific? → Fine-tuned model. This alone cut one company's AI costs by 60% while improving response quality. Use the right tool for each job.
key_points: Don't use one model for everything
Classifier routes requests
Small model handles 70% of queries
Large model for 30% complex queries
Can cut costs by 60%

knowledge_base.topic: Async Processing
knowledge_base.content: If you're running AI synchronously, you're doing it wrong. Why wait for the model when you can queue it? Async processing pattern: User makes request, You immediately return task ID, Process AI in background, User polls or gets webhook, Return results when ready. Benefits: No timeout issues, Better user experience, Can batch requests, Handle load spikes, Retry failed jobs easily. For long-running AI tasks (video generation, large document processing), async is mandatory. Add a job queue (Redis, RabbitMQ, SQS) and process AI requests in workers. Show users progress, not loading screens.
key_points: Don't run AI synchronously
Return task ID immediately
Process in background
No timeout issues
Better for long-running tasks
Use job queues: Redis, RabbitMQ, SQS

knowledge_base.topic: Fine-tuning vs Prompting
knowledge_base.content: Everyone asks: should I fine-tune or just use better prompts? Here's the truth most people won't tell you: Start with prompting. Always. Fine-tuning is for when: You have 1000+ high-quality examples, Your task is very specific, Prompting doesn't work after extensive testing, You need consistent formatting, Cost and latency matter significantly. Stick with prompting when: Your task changes frequently, You don't have quality training data, You need flexibility, Your use case is standard. I've seen companies waste $50k on fine-tuning when better prompts would've solved it. Test extensively with prompts first. Fine-tune only when you hit a clear ceiling.
key_points: Start with prompting always
Fine-tune when you have 1000+ examples
Fine-tune for very specific tasks
Companies waste $50k on unnecessary fine-tuning
Test prompts extensively first

knowledge_base.topic: Logging and Observability
knowledge_base.content: If you're not logging AI interactions, you're flying blind. What you must log: Every input and output, Model version and parameters, Response time and token usage, User feedback and corrections, Errors and edge cases. Why this matters: Debug production issues, Find improvement opportunities, Track costs accurately, Build fine-tuning datasets, Analyze user patterns. Set up dashboards for: Request volume over time, Average response time, Error rates by type, Cost per request, User satisfaction metrics. Tools: Langfuse, LangSmith, Helicone, or build custom. You can't improve what you don't measure.
key_points: Log every input and output
Track model version and parameters
Monitor response time and token usage
Build dashboards for key metrics
Tools: Langfuse, LangSmith, Helicone

knowledge_base.topic: Security and Prompt Injection
knowledge_base.content: Your AI is vulnerable to attacks. Here's how hackers exploit it: Prompt injection - users try to override your instructions, Data exfiltration - tricking AI to reveal system prompts, Jailbreaking - bypassing safety guidelines. Defense strategies: Input sanitization - strip suspicious patterns, Instruction hierarchy - make system prompts unoverridable, Output filtering - detect and block leaked instructions, Sandboxing - limit what AI can access. Never put secrets in prompts. Never trust user input completely. Always validate AI outputs. Add rate limiting per user. Monitor for attack patterns. Security in AI isn't optional. It's day-one requirement.
key_points: Prompt injection attacks are real
Input sanitization strips suspicious patterns
Make system prompts unoverridable
Never put secrets in prompts
Security is day-one requirement

knowledge_base.topic: Testing and Evaluation
knowledge_base.content: You can't ship AI without proper testing. But testing AI is different from testing code. You need: Unit tests - for deterministic components, Integration tests - for full workflows, LLM-as-judge - AI evaluating AI outputs, Human evaluation - for quality and edge cases, A/B testing - for production validation. Build eval sets: 50+ diverse examples, Include edge cases, Cover failure modes, Update regularly. Test for: Correctness, Consistency, Safety, Speed, Cost. Run evals on every prompt change. Track scores over time. Regression testing is critical - new prompts can break old use cases.
key_points: Testing AI differs from testing code
Build eval sets with 50+ examples
Use LLM-as-judge for evaluation
Test for: correctness, consistency, safety, speed, cost
Run evals on every prompt change

knowledge_base.topic: Model Versioning
knowledge_base.content: Your AI model just got updated. Now what? Without version control, you're in trouble. Every production system needs: Pin exact model versions, Test updates in staging first, Gradual rollout strategy, Rollback capability. When new model drops: Test against your eval set, Run A/B test with 5% traffic, Monitor metrics closely, Gradually increase to 100%, Keep old version ready. Why this matters: Model updates can break your prompts, Performance might regress on your use case, Costs could change dramatically. Never auto-update models in production. Never assume new = better. Always validate before full deployment.
key_points: Pin exact model versions
Test updates in staging first
Gradual rollout: 5% → 100%
Keep rollback capability
Never auto-update in production

knowledge_base.topic: RAG Architecture Explained: When to Use It and When Not To
knowledge_base.content: RAG isn't a default. It's a decision. The real question is: do you need retrieval, or do you just need better prompts? A good architecture explanation covers the decision tree and the three common tiers: naive RAG (simple top-k vector retrieval), advanced RAG (better chunking, reranking, hybrid search), and agentic RAG (tools + iterative retrieval + planning). The goal is not "add RAG"—it's to reduce hallucinations while keeping latency and cost within budget.
key_points: Decide: prompt engineering vs retrieval first
Naive RAG: simple vector top-k retrieval
Advanced RAG: chunking + rerank + hybrid search
Agentic RAG: iterative retrieval with tools/plans
Trade-offs: accuracy vs latency vs cost

knowledge_base.topic: How to Architect AI Agents: Design Patterns That Actually Work
knowledge_base.content: Agentic systems aren't "chatbots with tools"—they're workflows. A solid agent architecture breaks down patterns like ReAct (reason + act), tool use, explicit planning, reflection loops, and multi-agent orchestration. The key is knowing when not to use an agent: if a single grounded LLM call (or even cached answer) solves it reliably, an agent is overhead. If tasks require multi-step tool calls, verification, or long-running workflows, agents win.
key_points: Agents are workflow architectures, not prompts
ReAct: interleave reasoning and tool actions
Add explicit planning and reflection where needed
Use multi-agent orchestration for complex pipelines
Know the cutoff: single call vs agent system

knowledge_base.topic: The AI Cost Architecture: How to Reduce LLM Costs by 90%
knowledge_base.content: Cost reduction is architecture, not discounts. Combine tiered model strategy (expensive model for planning, cheap model for execution), prompt caching, batching, and structured outputs to reduce tokens and retries. Add a build-vs-buy decision for inference: sometimes self-hosting makes sense, sometimes APIs are cheaper when you include ops costs. The best cost systems are intentional: they route, cache, compress context, and avoid unnecessary LLM calls.
key_points: Tiered models: plan with strong model, execute with cheap model
Prompt caching architecture to avoid repeat calls
Batching requests to reduce overhead and cost
Structured outputs reduce tokens and retries
Decide: self-host vs API based on true total cost

knowledge_base.topic: Production AI Architecture: From MVP to Scale
knowledge_base.content: Scaling AI isn't just "add a bigger model." A production architecture includes an API gateway, a model abstraction layer, a caching layer, fallback chains, observability, and rate limiting. Streaming UX makes systems feel faster; fallbacks prevent outages from becoming product failures. The difference between a demo and a real system is operational: guardrails, monitoring, and predictable behavior under load.
key_points: Add an API gateway for control and policy enforcement
Use a model abstraction layer to avoid vendor lock-in
Design fallback chains (cache → smaller model → human)
Observability is mandatory: latency, cost, quality
Streaming UX improves perceived performance

knowledge_base.topic: Security Architecture for AI Apps (Vibe-Coded or Not)
knowledge_base.content: AI apps expand your attack surface. You need a threat model that includes prompt injection, data exfiltration, PII leakage, rate limiting, authentication, and output sanitization. If you're building fast (or vibe-coding), security must still be architectural: validate inputs, sanitize outputs, minimize tool permissions, and monitor for abuse patterns. Treat AI endpoints like money endpoints.
key_points: Threat model: injection, exfiltration, PII leakage
Validate inputs and sanitize outputs by default
Least-privilege tool access and sandboxing
Rate limiting + auth are non-negotiable
Monitor for abuse and anomalous traffic patterns

knowledge_base.topic: Prompt Engineering Architecture: Version, Test, Deploy
knowledge_base.content: Prompting is now an engineering system. Treat prompts as code: version control, A/B testing in production, evaluation pipelines, prompt registries, and deployment strategies. This makes quality measurable and repeatable. The core idea: you don't "ship a prompt"—you ship a prompt lifecycle with regression tests, rollout, and rollback.
key_points: Prompts as code: version control and change history
A/B test prompts with real traffic
Evaluation pipelines for regressions and edge cases
Prompt registries to manage environments and ownership
Deploy prompts with staged rollouts and rollback

knowledge_base.topic: MCP (Model Context Protocol) Explained: The USB-C of AI
knowledge_base.content: MCP standardizes how models discover and call tools. Architecturally, it’s a client-server protocol: MCP servers expose tools, MCP clients discover and invoke them, and agents use those tools as capabilities. Understanding MCP helps you design extensible systems (swap tools without rewriting prompts), but it also introduces security considerations: tool permissions, data boundaries, and safe tool execution.
key_points: MCP connects agents to tools via standard interfaces
Servers expose tools; clients discover and invoke
Enables composable, swap-in/swap-out toolchains
Compare MCP vs A2A (tools vs agent communication)
Security: permissions, boundaries, safe execution

knowledge_base.topic: Context Engineering: The Skill That Replaced Prompt Engineering
knowledge_base.content: Most failures aren't prompt failures—they're context failures. Context engineering focuses on designing the context pipeline: what information enters the model, what gets excluded, how context is summarized, and how memory is stored and retrieved. Great context systems enforce relevance, reduce token waste, and improve reliability more than endlessly tweaking instructions.
key_points: Design a context pipeline, not just a prompt
Choose what to include/exclude explicitly
Use summaries and retrieval instead of stuffing
Memory systems: short-term vs long-term strategies
Better context usually beats more prompting

knowledge_base.topic: AI Architecture for Startups: Build vs Buy vs API
knowledge_base.content: Startups win by making the right AI build decisions. The architecture question: do you build models, buy tools, or use APIs? A decision framework should weigh data moat, costs, vendor lock-in, time-to-market, and ops burden. Many teams start with APIs, then move to fine-tuning or self-hosting only when usage, data, or unit economics justify it.
key_points: Use a decision framework: data moat, cost, speed
APIs are best for fast iteration and early stage
Beware vendor lock-in and switching costs
Hybrid architectures are common in practice
Move to custom/self-host when unit economics demand

knowledge_base.topic: How AI Coding Tools Actually Work: Architecture Deep Dive
knowledge_base.content: AI coding tools aren’t magic—they’re context pipelines plus ranking. They gather context from the repo, recent edits, symbols, tests, and sometimes tool calls (e.g., via MCP). Then they decide between autocomplete vs agentic workflows: autocomplete predicts next tokens; agents plan, call tools, and iterate. Understanding this architecture helps you use these tools effectively and safely.
key_points: Core: context gathering + model inference + ranking
Autocomplete vs agentic coding are different products
Context quality controls output quality
MCP/tool integrations expand capabilities
Safety: avoid leaking secrets via context/tooling

knowledge_base.topic: Multi-Agent Architecture: How AI Teams Work Together
knowledge_base.content: Multi-agent systems split roles: a supervisor plans and delegates; workers execute specialized tasks; shared memory coordinates progress. This architecture helps when tasks are too complex for one loop or require parallelism (support, content pipelines, dev workflows). The key is orchestration: communication protocol, memory sharing, and clear contracts between agents.
key_points: Supervisor-worker pattern is the default multi-agent setup
Use orchestration for delegation and coordination
Define communication and contracts between agents
Share memory safely across agents when needed
Don’t over-agent: single-agent is often enough

knowledge_base.topic: AI Observability: How to Monitor LLMs in Production
knowledge_base.content: Shipping AI is the start, not the finish. You need observability across latency, cost, quality, hallucination rate, and failures. Trace multi-step pipelines end-to-end, evaluate outputs in production, detect drift, and build dashboards that show where quality breaks. Without this, you're guessing why users churn or costs spike.
key_points: Track metrics: latency, cost, quality, hallucination rate
Trace the full pipeline (tools + retrieval + LLM calls)
Run evaluation in production, not only offline
Detect drift as data/users change
Dashboards turn AI from art into engineering

knowledge_base.topic: GraphRAG and Knowledge Graphs for AI: When and Why
knowledge_base.content: Vector RAG struggles with relational queries. GraphRAG pairs retrieval with knowledge graphs so the system can answer questions that depend on entities and relationships. The architecture choice is about query type: similarity search vs relational reasoning. Many real systems use both: vector retrieval for context + graph queries for structured relationships.
key_points: Vector RAG fails on deep relational questions
Knowledge graphs represent entities and relations explicitly
GraphRAG combines graph reasoning with retrieval
Use both vector + graph when queries demand it
Choose architecture based on query patterns

knowledge_base.topic: AI Guardrails Architecture: How to Make AI Safe for Business
knowledge_base.content: Business-safe AI needs layered guardrails: input/output validation, content filtering, PII detection pipelines, compliance checks, toxicity and brand safety filters. The trick is building guardrails without killing performance: fast checks first, heavier checks only on risky outputs, and clear fallback paths when validation fails.
key_points: Layer guardrails: validate, filter, detect PII, comply
Use risk-based routing for expensive checks
Protect brand safety and customer trust
Design fallbacks for blocked or unsafe outputs
Measure guardrail impact on latency and quality

knowledge_base.topic: The 3-Layer AI Architecture Every App Needs
knowledge_base.content: Most AI apps start as a single API call. The scalable architecture has three layers: Gateway (policy, auth, routing), Processing (pipelines, caching, guardrails), and Intelligence (models, tools, retrieval). This separation makes systems easier to evolve, cheaper to run, and safer to operate.
key_points: Gateway layer: auth, rate limits, routing, policy
Processing layer: caching, guardrails, pipelines
Intelligence layer: models, tools, retrieval
Separation enables iteration and reliability
Prevents one-off prompt spaghetti architectures

knowledge_base.topic: Stop Using Temperature 0.7 for Everything
knowledge_base.content: Temperature isn't a default—it’s a control knob for variance. You should choose it based on the failure mode you can tolerate: deterministic outputs for production workflows vs creative exploration for ideation. A practical rule: lower temperature for structured outputs and tool calls; higher temperature for brainstorming and content generation.
key_points: Temperature controls randomness/variance
Lower for structured outputs and tool execution
Higher for ideation and exploration
Pick based on risk tolerance, not habit
Stability beats creativity in production systems

knowledge_base.topic: The AI Startup Stack for Under $100/month
knowledge_base.content: You can build a production-grade AI MVP without enterprise infra. A lean stack might be: Vercel for hosting, Supabase for data, a single LLM API for intelligence, and basic observability. The key is architecture discipline: caching, rate limits, and fallbacks—so you don’t pay for mistakes at scale.
key_points: Lean infra can still be production-minded
Pick simple managed services early
Add caching and rate limits from day one
Instrument basic observability immediately
Design fallbacks to avoid total outages

knowledge_base.topic: Why Your AI App Needs a Circuit Breaker
knowledge_base.content: If your AI provider goes down, your app shouldn't die with it. A circuit breaker prevents cascading failures by detecting repeated upstream errors and temporarily stopping calls, forcing fallbacks instead. Combine it with retries, exponential backoff, and fallback chains (cache → smaller model → human) for real resilience.
key_points: Circuit breaker stops cascades during provider outages
Use thresholds + cool-down windows
Combine with retries and exponential backoff
Route to fallbacks when breaker is open
Protects reliability and cost under incident conditions

knowledge_base.topic: The Difference Between AI Wrappers and AI Products
knowledge_base.content: Wrappers are thin UI over an API call. Products compound value: they have proprietary data, repeatable workflows, and feedback loops that improve over time. Architecturally, products build systems around AI: retrieval, memory, tools, guardrails, and domain-specific pipelines.
key_points: Wrappers: UI + single model call
Products: workflows + data + compounding value
Proprietary data is a moat
Pipelines and memory differentiate experiences
Feedback loops turn usage into improvement

knowledge_base.topic: AI Latency Budget: How to Hit Sub-2-Second Responses
knowledge_base.content: Speed is an architecture constraint. To hit sub-2s, you need streaming, caching, smart model selection, and response chunking. Build a latency budget per step (retrieval, tool calls, model time, validation) and design the pipeline so the user sees progress immediately, not after the whole response completes.
key_points: Set a latency budget for every pipeline stage
Use streaming to reduce perceived latency
Cache common queries and intermediate results
Route simple tasks to faster models
Chunk outputs and show progress early

knowledge_base.topic: Function Calling vs Tool Use vs MCP: What is the Difference?
knowledge_base.content: People mix these terms and build messy systems. Function calling is a model feature for producing structured calls. Tool use is the broader architecture pattern: models invoking external capabilities with permissions and validation. MCP is a standard protocol for defining and discovering tools across servers and clients. Knowing the difference helps you design clean, composable AI stacks.
key_points: Function calling: structured call outputs from the model
Tool use: architecture pattern for invoking capabilities
MCP: standard protocol for tool discovery/invocation
Permissions and validation are part of real tool use
Clean abstractions reduce prompt and infra chaos

knowledge_base.topic: Embeddings Explained Like You Are 5
knowledge_base.content: Embeddings turn words into numbers so machines can compare meaning. They're the foundation of semantic search and RAG: you embed text into vectors, then retrieve the closest chunks for a query. If your retrieval is bad, your model will still hallucinate—because it’s answering from weak context.
key_points: Embeddings map text to vectors (numbers)
Similarity search retrieves semantically related chunks
Embeddings power RAG and semantic caches
Retrieval quality controls answer quality
Bad context leads to hallucinations even with good models

knowledge_base.topic: Why 90% of AI MVPs Fail After Launch
knowledge_base.content: AI MVPs often work in demos and collapse in production. The missing pieces are operational: error handling, fallbacks, monitoring, rate limits, and user feedback loops. The architecture must assume failure: models are slow, wrong, or down, and users will push edge cases immediately.
key_points: Demos hide real-world edge cases and abuse
Add error handling and fallbacks early
Monitoring is required to debug and improve
Rate limits protect you from runaway cost
Feedback loops improve quality post-launch

knowledge_base.topic: The Real Cost of an AI API Call (Breakdown)
knowledge_base.content: The true cost isn't just tokens. It's input tokens, output tokens, retries on failures, latency cost (users churn), and the engineering overhead of guardrails and monitoring. A cost architecture breaks costs down per stage and actively reduces them with caching, routing, batching, and structured outputs.
key_points: Token cost = input + output (but not the full story)
Retries and failures multiply costs silently
Latency has a real conversion/churn cost
Guardrails and observability add overhead
Architecture reduces cost: caching, routing, batching

knowledge_base.topic: AI Memory Systems: Short-Term vs Long-Term vs Episodic
knowledge_base.content: Production AI memory isn't "send the whole chat history." Memory is designed: short-term memory for the current task, long-term memory for stable user facts/preferences, and episodic memory for specific past events. The architecture decides what gets stored, how it's retrieved, and how it’s kept safe and up-to-date.
key_points: Short-term memory: current session/task context
Long-term memory: stable user facts and preferences
Episodic memory: past events and interactions
Retrieval beats stuffing history into context
Memory needs privacy, expiry, and validation rules

knowledge_base.topic: The Webhook Pattern for AI Agents
knowledge_base.content: Polling is expensive and brittle. Webhook-based agents wait for events (payment success, ticket update, webhook triggers) and then act. This enables real-time workflows, reduces cost, and makes long-running automations reliable. Architect it with idempotency, deduplication, and clear state transitions.
key_points: Event-driven agents outperform polling-based agents
Webhooks enable real-time, low-cost workflows
Design for idempotency and deduplication
Use queues/workers for reliability
Track state transitions to avoid double actions

knowledge_base.topic: Evaluation-Driven Development for AI Apps
knowledge_base.content: Write evals before prompts. This is the TDD equivalent for AI: define what "good" looks like using an evaluation set, then iterate prompts and pipelines until you pass. Run evals on every change so you don’t regress silently when a prompt update improves one case and breaks another.
key_points: Define evals first: correctness, safety, quality
Build an eval set with real-world edge cases
Run evals on every prompt or pipeline change
Track scores over time to prevent regressions
Use human + LLM-as-judge where appropriate

knowledge_base.topic: Fine-Tuning vs RAG vs Prompt Engineering: Decision Tree
knowledge_base.content: There are three levers, but one decision: what’s your bottleneck? Prompt engineering is cheapest and fastest. RAG helps when knowledge is external and changing. Fine-tuning helps when you need consistent formatting, domain behavior, or style—and you have enough high-quality examples. The best teams use a decision tree, not opinions.
key_points: Start with prompting for speed and flexibility
Use RAG for external, dynamic knowledge needs
Fine-tune when you have enough quality examples
Choose based on bottleneck: knowledge vs behavior
Use a decision tree to avoid wasted effort

knowledge_base.topic: What Happens When You Hit an AI API Rate Limit
knowledge_base.content: Rate limits are predictable failures, so your architecture should plan for them. Handle rate limiting with queuing, exponential backoff, fallback models/providers, and graceful degradation. If you don't, one traffic spike turns into crashes, retries, and a cost spiral.
key_points: Expect rate limits and design for them
Queue requests and apply backpressure
Use exponential backoff instead of aggressive retries
Fail over to fallback models/providers when possible
Graceful degradation protects UX and cost

twitter_post_types.name: Problem → Solution Hook
twitter_post_types.description: Start with shocking problem, build urgency, present clear solution

characteristics.best_for: Technical solutions, cost savings, performance improvements

twitter_post_types.name: List/Thread Starter
twitter_post_types.description: Promise specific number of items, progressive disclosure, thread-friendly

characteristics.length: 10-15 tweets for thread, 8-10 lines for single post

twitter_post_types.name: Narrative/Story
twitter_post_types.description: Personal experience or case study with concrete numbers and revelation

twitter_post_types.name: Contrarian/Hot Take
twitter_post_types.description: Challenge conventional wisdom, present unpopular but valuable truth

characteristics.best_for: Debunking myths, sharing insider knowledge, thought leadership

elements_to_include: Evidence or examples supporting your view
examples: The truth about X that nobody talks about

twitter_post_types.name: Mistake/Warning
twitter_post_types.description: Call out common mistake, show consequences, provide correction

characteristics.engagement_driver: Fear of making mistake + desire to do it right

examples: I see this mistake kill startups every week
Stop doing X before it's too late
